pattern: Enumerate palindromic square roots by constructing palindromes from their shorter first-halves, square each, check if the square is also a palindrome and falls in range.
hints: Instead of scanning every integer in [L, R] to test if it is a palindrome square, work backwards: enumerate palindromes p, compute p^2, and test whether p^2 is itself a palindrome in range.
A palindrome is determined by its first ceil(len/2) digits; generate all odd-length and even-length palindromes by iterating over all possible first-halves, then mirror them.
The square root of 10^18 is 10^9, so you need palindromes up to 10^9; iterate first-halves from 1 to 100000 (giving both even and odd length palindromes up to ~10^9), square each, and check.

edge_inputs.description: range 1 to 9: single digits 1,4,9 are all super-palindromes
676 is a palindrome but 26 is not, so 676 must not be counted
range up to 10000 to capture 9801 (=99^2, but 9801 is not a palindrome) and nearby
full range near the top of constraints
exact boundary where left equals a known super-palindrome

stress.generator_python:
def gen(rng, size):
    a = rng.randint(1, 10**17)
    b = rng.randint(a, min(a * 1000, 10**18 - 1))
    return [str(a), str(b)]
